attributes.stem: A 28-year-old woman presents to the Emergency Department at 16 weeks gestation with a three-day history of fever and a widespread vesicular rash. She has no significant medical history and takes only pregnancy vitamins. She reports no known contact with chickenpox but has a three-year-old child who attends daycare. On examination, she appears well. Her pulse rate is 88/min, blood pressure 110/70 mmHg, respiratory rate 16/min, SpO2 98% on room air, and temperature 37.8°C. She has a generalised vesicular rash in various stages of development. Two weeks later, ultrasonography reveals evidence of fetal infection.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely mode of transmission to the fetus?
attributes.explanation:
This case demonstrates varicella (chickenpox) infection in pregnancy with subsequent fetal infection. Vertical transmission occurs when the varicella zoster virus crosses the placenta during maternal viraemia, potentially leading to congenital varicella syndrome. This is most concerning when infection occurs between 8-20 weeks gestation, as in this case.

Sexual transmission is not a recognised mode of transmission for varicella zoster virus. This mode of transmission is more commonly associated with infections such as HIV, hepatitis B, and herpes simplex virus.

Direct transmission refers to transmission through direct contact with lesions or contaminated objects, but this is not how the virus reaches the fetus in utero.

Droplet transmission is how the virus typically spreads between individuals through respiratory secretions, but this is not how the virus reaches the fetus in utero.

Airborne transmission can occur between individuals due to the highly contagious nature of varicella zoster virus, but this is not the mechanism by which the fetus becomes infected.

The risk of congenital varicella syndrome is approximately 2% when maternal infection occurs in the first 20 weeks of pregnancy. Features may include skin scarring, limb hypoplasia, neurological abnormalities, and eye defects. If maternal infection occurs within 5 days before to 2 days after delivery, severe neonatal varicella may develop due to the absence of protective maternal antibodies.
learningPoints: Varicella zoster virus can cross the placenta and cause congenital varicella syndrome, particularly if maternal infection occurs between 8-20 weeks gestation.
The risk of congenital varicella syndrome is approximately 2% if maternal infection occurs in the first 20 weeks of pregnancy.
Neonatal varicella can occur if maternal infection occurs within 5 days before to 2 days after delivery.
references: Australasian Society for Infectious Diseases (ASID). Management of perinatal infections. East Melbourne: ASID; 2022 [cited 2024 Nov 5]. Available from: https://ranzcog.edu.au/wp-content/uploads/2022/05/ASID-Management-of-Perinatal-Infections.pdf
